executive_summary: The bot took only 2 trades this week, both losses, continuing a catastrophic pattern: 5 trades over 3 weeks with 0 wins and ₹-4994 cumulative loss. The Supply & Demand Zone strategy is fundamentally broken in its current form — it either refuses to trade (weeks of paralysis) or takes losing trades when filters are loosened. The strategy needs to be either completely overhauled or replaced with a complementary approach. Regime was predominantly trending_down (5/7 days) where S&D zones inherently underperform.
regime_assessment: Regime detection appears to be working correctly (5 trending_down days followed by 2 trending_up days aligns with market behavior), but the strategy has zero edge in ANY regime. Both trades taken during trending_down were losses, and no trades were generated during trending_up days either. The regime signal is not being used to meaningfully filter or adapt entries.

symbol_changes.remove_reason: No symbol data available to make informed decisions — this itself is a structural gap that needs fixing
symbol_changes.focus_reason: Cannot determine focus list without symbol-level P&L tracking. Recommend adding symbol logging as priority fix.

dow_pattern: Insufficient data — only 2 trades (Thursday and Wednesday, both losses). No actionable day-of-week pattern with this sample size.

param_search_adjustments.reasoning: The current RR of 2.0 means targets are rarely hit in trending markets. Lowering RR to 1.2-2.0 allows faster exits in profit. Lowering min_score floor to 60 increases trade frequency for data collection, but the real problem is structural — parameters alone won't fix a strategy that has 0% win rate across all conditions.

structural_recommendations.change: ADD a trend-following strategy as primary for trending_down/trending_up regimes. Supply & Demand zones are mean-reversion by nature and fundamentally conflict with trending regimes. Implement a simple breakout/breakdown strategy (e.g., 20-period high/low break with ATR trailing stop) that activates when regime is trending.
structural_recommendations.expected_impact: Trending regimes are 70%+ of observed days. A basic trend-following approach should yield 40-50% win rate with >2:1 RR, producing positive expectancy vs current 0%.

structural_recommendations.change: Implement symbol-level performance logging. Every trade must record: symbol, entry zone score, regime at entry, time of day, and outcome. Without this data, weekly reviews cannot identify which symbols or conditions produce wins.
structural_recommendations.expected_impact: Enables data-driven symbol selection within 2-3 weeks, potentially improving win rate by 10-15% through elimination of consistently losing symbols.

structural_recommendations.change: Add a regime-gate that BLOCKS Supply & Demand zone entries during trending_down unless the trade is WITH the trend (i.e., only short entries at supply zones). Currently appears the strategy may be taking long entries at demand zones in downtrends, which is counter-trend and explains 0% win rate.
structural_recommendations.expected_impact: Should eliminate at least 50% of losing trades by filtering out counter-trend entries. If both losses were longs in a downtrend, this alone would have saved ₹1999.

structural_recommendations.change: Implement a paper-trade mode that simulates entries without capital risk for the first 20 trades after any parameter change. Use this to validate edge exists before going live. The current approach of live-testing parameter changes is burning capital on unvalidated configurations.
structural_recommendations.expected_impact: Preserves capital during strategy validation. Would have saved ₹4994 over last 3 weeks while still collecting performance data.

structural_recommendations.change: Reduce position size by 50% until win rate exceeds 40% over a rolling 10-trade window. Current approach risks full position on a strategy with demonstrated 0% win rate.
structural_recommendations.expected_impact: Limits maximum weekly drawdown to ₹-1000 instead of ₹-2000 while strategy is being fixed.
